h1Title: Myofascial Pain Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Myofascial pain syndrome is a regional musculoskeletal pain disorder caused by hyperirritable foci within skeletal muscle — called trigger points — that produce local and referred pain patterns, restricted range of motion, and autonomic phenomena. It is among the most prevalent diagnoses in outpatient pain and rehabilitation clinics, accounting for 21–93% of patients presenting with musculoskeletal pain depending on the clinical setting. The trapezius, levator scapulae, and paraspinal muscles are the most commonly affected. Unlike fibromyalgia, myofascial pain is regional rather than widespread and is associated with discrete, palpable taut bands. Trigger-point injections with local anaesthetic, dry needling, manual therapy, and corrective exercise produce measurable relief.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 21–93% of musculoskeletal pain clinic patients carry a myofascial pain diagnosis; affects an estimated 44 million Americans at any given time
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages and sexes; slightly higher prevalence in women; most prevalent ages 27–50; sedentary office workers and athletes both at high risk
keyStats.avgAge: Peak clinical presentation ages 31–50; work-related myofascial pain most common at ages 27–55
keyStats.globalCases: Global prevalence uncertain; a 2020 systematic review estimated myofascial trigger points present in 49–89% of subjects with regional musculoskeletal pain conditions worldwide

definition: Myofascial pain syndrome (MPS; ICD-10: M79.1) is a chronic musculoskeletal pain condition defined by the presence of myofascial trigger points (MTrPs) — hyperirritable, palpable nodules within taut bands of skeletal muscle fibers that produce predictable local pain, referred pain, and a local twitch response on stimulation. Travell and Simons systematically mapped trigger points and their referred pain patterns in the landmark two-volume Myofascial Pain and Dysfunction text (1983, 1992), providing the clinical foundation still used in practice. The hypothesized mechanism involves dysfunctional motor endplates releasing excess acetylcholine, causing sustained muscle fiber contraction, localized ischemia, and a neurogenic inflammatory cascade — the 'energy crisis' hypothesis confirmed in part by elevated substance P and low pH in trigger-point loci. Referred pain — pain experienced away from the trigger point itself — is mediated by central sensitization mechanisms and explains why cervical trigger points cause frontal headache, upper trapezius trigger points cause temporal headache, and gluteal trigger points cause pseudo-sciatica. MPS is distinct from fibromyalgia (widespread, generalized pain) and from local myalgia (non-specific muscle soreness without the taut band and referral). Physical medicine and rehabilitation specialists, pain medicine physicians, and physiotherapists are the primary providers.

typesClassification.description: Spontaneously painful at rest and on palpation; produces the patient's familiar referred pain pattern. Active trigger points are the diagnostic and clinical target — they limit range of motion and produce functional disability.

Palpable taut band with local tenderness on palpation but no spontaneous pain. Latent trigger points restrict range of motion and can be activated to active status by mechanical overload, psychological stress, or systemic illness.

A trigger point that develops within the referred pain zone of an active trigger point. Resolving the primary trigger point typically resolves the satellite, but failure to identify the satellite may leave residual referred pain after primary treatment.

Located at the musculotendinous junction or bone-tendon interface rather than the mid-belly. Associated with enthesopathy and often produces more localized mechanical pain than a central trigger point.

primarySymptoms: Regional, dull, aching muscular pain that the patient recognises as their 'usual' pain pattern when the trigger point is pressed — the pathognomonic referred-pain reproduction sign.
Palpable taut band within the muscle belly — a rope-like firmness appreciated on flat or pincer palpation, distinct from the surrounding relaxed muscle.
A local twitch response — a brief, visible muscle fasciculation when the taut band is snapped transversely or needled — highly specific for an active trigger point.
Restricted range of motion in the muscle's direction of lengthening due to pain-limited stretch tolerance and taut band shortening.

Referred pain following predictable regional patterns: upper trapezius to temporal headache; infraspinatus to anterior shoulder and arm; quadratus lumborum to sacroiliac and buttock; sternocleidomastoid to forehead and behind the ear.
Autonomic phenomena in the referred zone — pilomotor erection, local sweating, vasoconstriction causing skin temperature asymmetry, and tearing or nasal congestion with head and neck trigger points.
Tender-point allodynia — hyperalgesia within the referred zone that can mimic nerve entrapment or radiculopathy.
Sleep disturbance from positional aggravation of active trigger points, particularly cervical and upper trapezius trigger points during nocturnal shoulder elevation.
Fatigue and reduced work capacity proportional to the number of active trigger points, reflecting the metabolic cost of sustained tonic muscle activity in taut bands.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent, predictable muscle aching in the same regional location after specific activities or sustained postures — suggests a developing latent trigger point before it becomes persistently active.
Morning stiffness lasting under 30 minutes in one muscle group, particularly the upper trapezius or cervical paraspinals, that loosens with gentle movement.
Headache that reliably follows sustained computer use or neck extension and resolves with neck stretching — an early sign of active cervical or trapezius trigger points.
emergencySigns: Severe neck pain with sudden onset after minor activity combined with progressive upper-limb neurological deficits — must exclude acute cervical disc herniation or cord compression before attributing to myofascial origin.
Chest pain with trigger points in the pectoralis minor, scalene, or intercostal muscles — cardiac and pulmonary causes must be excluded before a myofascial diagnosis is applied.
New onset of widespread constant pain with fever, weight loss, or night sweats alongside regional muscle pain — exclude malignancy, inflammatory arthropathy, or infection.

causes.cause: Repetitive muscle overuse or sustained static loading
causes.description: Sustained low-level eccentric loading — such as holding a mouse with an elevated shoulder or maintaining cervical flexion for hours — prevents normal motor endplate recovery, promotes excess acetylcholine release, and initiates taut band formation. Office workers, surgeons, and musicians are disproportionately affected.

causes.cause: Acute muscle strain or direct trauma
causes.description: A single overload event (whiplash, heavy lift, fall) damages sarcomeres and triggers the endplate energy crisis cascade. Trigger points seeded at the time of injury can persist long after initial tissue healing if perpetuating factors are not addressed.

Forward head posture, upper crossed syndrome (tight pectorals and cervical extensors with weak deep neck flexors and lower trapezius), and lower crossed syndrome (tight hip flexors and erectors with weak glutei) sustain eccentric loading on specific muscle groups, perpetuating trigger points.

Elevated sympathetic tone and cortisol associated with chronic stress lower the activation threshold of trigger points, increase muscle co-contraction bracing, and worsen pain. Poor sleep reduces pain-modulation efficiency. Myofascial pain severity correlates with anxiety and depression scores in cross-sectional studies.

Nutritional deficiencies (vitamin D, B12, folate), hypothyroidism, and chronic viral infections lower the threshold for trigger point formation and impair treatment response. Travell and Simons identified metabolic perpetuating factors as among the most common causes of treatment failure.

causes.cause: Joint dysfunction with reflex muscle guarding
causes.description: Zygapophyseal joint arthrosis, sacroiliac joint dysfunction, and peripheral joint osteoarthritis trigger reflex muscle guarding via spinal facilitation, seeding satellite trigger points in the surrounding musculature. Treating the joint pathology reduces satellite trigger-point burden.

riskFactors.description: Sustained sitting with shoulder elevation or cervical flexion for more than 4 hours daily is among the strongest modifiable risk factors; ergonomic workstation redesign reduces trigger-point incidence by 30–50% in randomized workplace studies.

Reduced muscle aerobic capacity lowers the threshold for sustained motor endplate dysfunction during activity. Regular aerobic exercise is protective; sedentary lifestyle increases myofascial pain prevalence 2–3×.
25-OH vitamin D levels below 20 ng/mL are over-represented in myofascial pain patients; correction improves trigger-point treatment response in several prospective cohort studies.
Psychiatric comorbidity is found in 50–60% of chronic MPS patients; it lowers descending pain inhibitory tone, heightens central sensitization, and significantly predicts worse functional outcomes.
Disrupted sleep reduces growth hormone release and pain-threshold recovery; patients with comorbid obstructive sleep apnoea show higher trigger-point burden and slower response to treatment.
Cervical spondylosis, lumbar degenerative disc disease, and osteoarthritis generate reflex muscle guarding that seeds and perpetuates satellite trigger points in periarticular muscles.

riskFactors.factor: Previous muscle injury or repetitive strain
riskFactors.description: Scar tissue from prior muscle injury has reduced elastic compliance, increasing the mechanical load on adjacent fibers and predisposing to recurrent trigger-point activation.

affectedDemographics: Office and computer workers: sustained static postures produce cervical and upper trapezius trigger points at rates 3–5× higher than in physically active occupations; 60–80% of desk workers report at least one active trigger point on examination.
Athletes: overtraining and repetitive eccentric loading without adequate recovery produce trigger points in sport-specific muscle groups; prevalence in competitive athletes estimated at 55–85%.

Adults aged 27–50: peak working years combine highest occupational exposure with physiologic peak of sustained postural demand; MPS is the leading diagnosis in occupational rehabilitation in this age group.
Women: slightly higher trigger-point prevalence than men in clinical populations; hormonal variability, higher baseline pain sensitivity, and higher rates of fibromyalgia comorbidity contribute.
diagnosisOverview: Myofascial pain syndrome is diagnosed on physical examination. There is no confirmatory blood test, imaging study, or electrodiagnostic marker. The clinical criteria established by Simons, Travell, and Simons require: (1) the patient's complaint of regional pain, (2) a palpable taut band in the accessible skeletal muscle, (3) exquisite spot tenderness of a nodule within the taut band, and (4) reproduction of the patient's pain pattern on stimulation of the tender nodule. A local twitch response on snapping palpation or needling is a specific but not always obtainable finding. The referred pain map described by Travell and Simons guides examination — the clinician examines muscle groups known to refer pain to the patient's symptomatic region. Algometric pressure-pain threshold measurement (kg/cm²) with a handheld algometer documents tenderness severity and tracks treatment response. Imaging serves to exclude structural causes: cervical MRI excludes radiculopathy or myelopathy when upper limb symptoms accompany neck trigger points; lumbar MRI excludes herniated disc in low back and leg presentations; shoulder ultrasound and MRI exclude rotator cuff pathology when shoulder trigger points are suspected. Surface electromyography and ultrasound elastography can detect trigger-point associated spontaneous electrical activity and altered tissue stiffness respectively, but remain research tools rather than standard clinical tests. Distinguishing MPS from fibromyalgia requires identifying whether pain is regional (MPS) or widespread with generalized allodynia (fibromyalgia); the two conditions frequently coexist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies taut bands, tender nodules, local twitch response, and referred pain pattern reproduction — the basis of MPS diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician systematically palpates muscle groups using flat or pincer technique with 3–5 kg/cm² pressure. A local twitch or referred pain reproduction confirms an active trigger point. Takes 20–30 minutes for a regional examination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies trigger-point tenderness using a handheld algometer; tracks treatment response and documents pain sensitization severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is pressed over trigger-point sites and the patient indicates when mild pain begins. Values under 2 kg/cm² indicate significant sensitization. Quick, non-invasive, takes under 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes radiculopathy, myelopathy, disc herniation, and facet joint pathology when neurological symptoms accompany regional muscle pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minutes in the scanner. No contrast required for initial evaluation unless cord compression is suspected. No discomfort beyond lying still.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes myofascial referred pain from true peripheral nerve entrapment or radiculopathy in patients with distal symptoms or weakness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrical pulses applied to the skin and fine-needle EMG of selected muscles. Takes 45–60 minutes. Mild discomfort. EMG shows normal motor unit architecture in pure myofascial pain.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, ESR, thyroid function, vitamin D, B12
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for perpetuating metabolic factors — hypothyroidism, anaemia, and nutritional deficiencies that sustain trigger points and limit treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw. Results in 1–2 days. Vitamin D below 20 ng/mL and hypothyroidism are the most commonly identified actionable findings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates increased tissue stiffness and reduced shear-wave velocity within taut bands compared with adjacent normal muscle; may guide needle placement in obese patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound probe placed over the muscle; computer analyses tissue stiffness in real time. Non-invasive. Available in specialist musculoskeletal imaging centres.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of myofascial pain syndrome targets trigger-point inactivation, correction of perpetuating factors, and restoration of normal muscle function and posture. Current evidence and practice guidelines from the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) and American Pain Society support a multimodal rehabilitation approach. Trigger-point injections with local anaesthetic (1% procaine or 0.5% bupivacaine, 0.5–1 mL per site) are the most rapidly effective inactivation technique; randomized trials show superiority over placebo injection for active trigger points in the trapezius and cervical region. Dry needling — the same needle technique without injectate — achieves comparable results when performed by trained physiotherapists or acupuncturists, with a 2020 meta-analysis showing significant VAS reduction of 1.5–2 points vs. sham. Botulinum toxin type A (BTX-A) injection into refractory trigger points provides 3–4 months of relief in cases resistant to repeat local anaesthetic injection; evidence quality is moderate. Manual therapy modalities — ischaemic compression, spray-and-stretch (cold vapocoolant applied to the skin followed by passive stretch), muscle energy technique, and Graston technique — inactivate trigger points through mechanical disruption of the taut band and improved blood flow. Structured exercise programs combining aerobic conditioning (30 minutes, 3× weekly), eccentric loading, and postural correction address the underlying muscle deconditioning and postural dysfunction that perpetuate trigger points. Psychological management — CBT, relaxation training, and sleep hygiene — reduces central sensitization and improves treatment durability. Perpetuating factors must be addressed concurrently: correct vitamin D deficiency with 2,000–4,000 IU daily, treat hypothyroidism, and optimize sleep. Multimodal programs that combine needling with manual therapy and exercise produce the most sustained outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Trigger-point injection (TPI) with local anaesthetic (1% procaine, 0.5–1 mL per site)
medicalTreatments.description: Needle is inserted into the palpated taut band and redirected in a fan pattern to elicit local twitch responses; local anaesthetic reduces post-injection soreness. Typically 3–5 sites per session; can repeat at 2–4 week intervals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: VAS pain reduction of 30–50% at 4 weeks vs. 10–20% for placebo injection in sham-controlled RCTs; best evidence for trapezius, cervical, and lumbar paraspinal trigger points

medicalTreatments.name: Dry needling (filiform acupuncture needle, no injectate)
medicalTreatments.description: Sterile filiform needle inserted into trigger point to elicit local twitch response; performed by trained physiotherapists or physiatrists. Equally effective as local anaesthetic injection in most comparative trials with less post-procedure soreness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 2020 meta-analysis (Morihisa et al.): significant VAS reduction of 1.5 points vs. sham at 4 weeks; moderate-quality evidence; superior to sham for upper-trapezius and neck trigger points

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin type A (BTX-A, 10–25 units per trigger point)
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into refractory trigger points to inhibit acetylcholine release at the dysfunctional motor endplate; effect lasts 12–16 weeks. Reserved for chronic MPS failing two or more local anaesthetic injection courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomized trials show 35–50% reduction in VAS pain at 8 weeks vs. 15–20% for saline in treatment-refractory upper trapezius and cervical trigger points

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg three times daily, 2–4 weeks) or topical diclofenac gel
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral or topical NSAIDs reduce peripheral inflammatory sensitization and allow physiotherapy participation with less procedural pain. Limited standalone efficacy for trigger-point resolution.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest VAS reduction as adjunct to needling; no evidence of sustained trigger-point inactivation as sole treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline 10–25 mg nocte) for central sensitization
medicalTreatments.description: Used in chronic MPS with established central sensitization, sleep disturbance, or comorbid depression. Improves sleep quality, raises pain threshold, and reduces muscle hyperalgesia at doses well below antidepressant levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Amitriptyline 25–75 mg nocte reduces pain VAS by 1–2 points vs. placebo at 8 weeks in chronic MPS with central sensitization; NNT approximately 4–5

alternativeTreatments.name: Spray-and-stretch (vapocoolant spray + passive muscle stretch)
alternativeTreatments.description: Application of ethyl chloride or fluoromethane spray to the skin over the trigger point and referral zone, followed by passive stretch to full range. A 2014 systematic review found the technique effective for short-term trigger-point deactivation and superior to passive stretch alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT)
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2016 systematic review of 8 RCTs found 830–980 nm LLLT applied to trigger points significantly reduced VAS pain and improved pressure-pain threshold compared with sham at 4 weeks, with effects most pronounced in the upper trapezius.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-frequency TENS (80–150 Hz) reduces pain in myofascial regions for 30–60 minutes post-application; useful as a daily home adjunct to physiotherapy and injection to enable exercise participation without acute pain escalation.

whySeeSpecialist: A physiatrist, pain medicine specialist, or physiotherapist with trigger-point expertise should evaluate patients who have not responded to 4–6 weeks of home stretching and activity modification, who require trigger-point injections, or who have recurrent MPS with suspected perpetuating factors such as hypothyroidism or sleep apnoea.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician trained in the Travell and Simons trigger-point examination methodology. For injection therapy, a physiatrist or pain physician with experience in landmark or ultrasound-guided regional injections is preferable. For rehabilitation, a physiotherapist with dry-needling certification and postural correction experience produces the most durable results.
hospitalCriteria: Physiotherapy department experienced in manual trigger-point therapy and dry needling
Pain clinic or physiatry service with trigger-point injection capability
Electromyography laboratory to exclude radiculopathy and nerve entrapment
Psychological services for CBT and pain-management programs
Laboratory for metabolic screening (vitamin D, thyroid function)
keyFacilities: Integrative medicine centres with trigger-point injection services
preventionStrategies: Redesign the workstation to ensure screen at eye level, elbows at 90°, and shoulders in neutral — eliminates the sustained upper-trapezius loading that seeds most cervical trigger points.
Take a 2-minute movement break every 30 minutes of sustained posture; shoulder rolls, neck retraction, and thoracic extension counteract the postural muscles most prone to trigger-point formation.

Maintain aerobic fitness (150 minutes moderate intensity weekly) — aerobic exercise reduces trigger-point sensitivity and raises pain thresholds through endorphin release and improved muscle oxygenation.
Screen and treat vitamin D deficiency — check 25-OH vitamin D annually and supplement to maintain levels above 30 ng/mL.
Address sleep disorders: 7–9 hours of restorative sleep restores pain-inhibitory mechanisms and reduces trigger-point reactivation rate.
Learn and perform a daily stretching routine for the muscle groups relevant to your occupation or sport — particularly upper trapezius, pectoralis minor, and hip flexors.
lifestyleModifications: Apply moist heat to affected muscle groups for 15 minutes before daily stretching to improve tissue extensibility and reduce trigger-point irritability.
Perform prescribed stretching program 2× daily — each major muscle group held at gentle tension for 60–90 seconds per stretch.
Use a lumbar support cushion for prolonged sitting and a rolled towel behind the cervical lordosis for sustained computer work.
Manage stress with structured daily relaxation (diaphragmatic breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation) — autonomic calming reduces resting trigger-point activity.
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule and treat sleep disorders — consult a physician if snoring, witnessed apnoeas, or non-restorative sleep are present.
Gradually increase daily step count to 8,000–10,000 steps; walking promotes longitudinal muscle load cycling that prevents static trigger-point perpetuation.

recommended: Vitamin D 2,000 IU daily if deficient (check serum level; dose to maintain 30–50 ng/mL) — supports muscle metabolic function and pain-threshold regulation
B-complex vitamins (especially B12 and folate) from eggs, dairy, leafy greens, and fortified foods — Travell and Simons identified B12 deficiency as a key perpetuating factor
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, legumes, dark chocolate) — cofactor in ATP synthesis and muscle relaxation at the sarcomere level
Anti-inflammatory omega-3 sources (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — reduce peripheral sensitization in muscle tissue
avoid: Excessive caffeine — raises muscle resting tone and sympathetic activation, lowering trigger-point activation threshold
High-sugar, low-nutrient processed foods that deplete B vitamins and magnesium
Alcohol in excess — disrupts sleep architecture and impairs muscle recovery and pain modulation
Very low-calorie diets that deplete muscle glycogen and increase pain sensitivity through hypoglycemia-associated sympathetic activation

exerciseGuidelines: Start with gentle aerobic activity (20–30 minutes walking or cycling daily) to improve muscle oxygenation without aggravating trigger points. Add a progressive stretching program for the primary affected muscle groups once acute pain is controlled. Eccentric-loading exercises (3 sets of 15 repetitions with Theraband) for the affected muscle groups improve tendon and muscle load tolerance. Resistance training and sport-specific training are reintroduced at 4–6 weeks after trigger points are inactivated, with emphasis on balanced antagonist training.
dailyManagement: Apply moist heat to trigger-point regions for 15 minutes each morning before beginning the day's activities.
Perform prescribed stretching routine twice daily — morning after heat and evening before sleep.
Use ergonomic equipment (wrist rests, lumbar support, adjustable monitor stand) continuously, not just during pain flares.
Take prescribed pain medication or muscle relaxant as directed — do not wait until pain escalates before taking a scheduled dose.
Track pain intensity and location daily on a body map; bring a completed 1-week pain diary to each clinical appointment.
Attend physiotherapy or dry-needling sessions as scheduled; 6–8 sessions over 4–6 weeks is a typical initial treatment course.
prognosis: Prognosis of myofascial pain syndrome depends critically on identifying and eliminating perpetuating factors. Acute MPS from a single precipitating event with no systemic perpetuators typically resolves within 4–8 weeks of combined injection and rehabilitative exercise. Chronic MPS (duration greater than 3 months) with multiple active trigger points and significant central sensitization follows a more variable course: approximately 50–60% of patients achieve sustained significant improvement with multimodal therapy, while 25–35% require ongoing periodic treatment and activity modification to maintain function. Negative prognostic indicators include duration above 12 months, comorbid fibromyalgia or depression, uncorrected vitamin D deficiency or hypothyroidism, and high catastrophizing scores on psychological screening. Occupational MPS resolves faster when temporary ergonomic accommodation is provided, with 70–80% return-to-work rates at 8–12 weeks of combined injection, physiotherapy, and ergonomic intervention.
recoveryTimeline: Acute MPS: 4–8 weeks with injection and physiotherapy. Chronic MPS: significant improvement at 3–6 months with multimodal treatment; full functional recovery may take 6–12 months. Perpetuating factors must be corrected first — vitamin D, thyroid, sleep — before predicting treatment duration.
complications: Chronic widespread pain and central sensitization — untreated or repeatedly triggered MPS can progress to fibromyalgia over years; risk heightened by sleep disorders and psychological comorbidity.

Post-injection soreness (delayed-onset muscle soreness for 24–48 hours after trigger-point injection or dry needling) — expected in most patients; managed with ice, gentle movement, and short-course NSAIDs.
Pneumothorax after dry needling of posterior intercostal muscles or periscapular trigger points — rare (incidence under 0.01%) but serious; prevented by strict needle length and angulation protocols.
Hematoma at injection sites — more common in patients on anticoagulants; compression for 2–3 minutes after needle withdrawal prevents most bruising.

Functional disability and work absence — in chronic MPS with multiple active trigger points, 10–15% of patients are temporarily disabled for work; early multimodal intervention is the most effective prevention.

Masking of a serious underlying diagnosis — trigger points can develop secondary to malignancy, fracture, or inflammatory arthropathy; regular reassessment and 'red flag' review are essential in patients not following expected progress.

supportResources.name: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke — Pain Information
supportResources.description: NIH-curated resource on chronic musculoskeletal pain mechanisms and evidence-based management.

supportResources.name: American Chronic Pain Association
supportResources.description: Patient-led organization offering pain management tools, peer support groups, and provider directories for chronic myofascial and musculoskeletal pain.

supportResources.name: Myopain Seminars — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Educational content from Travell and Simons-trained clinicians including trigger-point self-treatment guides and referral information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia is defined by widespread pain (bilateral, above and below the waist) affecting at least four regions plus fatigue and cognitive symptoms, with no discrete taut-band trigger points on examination. MPS is regional, has palpable taut bands producing specific referred pain patterns, and responds to local trigger-point treatment. The two conditions frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical Radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervical radiculopathy produces neurological deficits (motor weakness, reflex changes, dermatomal sensory loss) confirmed on MRI and nerve conduction studies. Myofascial referred pain can mimic dermatomal distributions but lacks true neurological signs, has normal nerve conduction, and responds to trigger-point treatment rather than spinal intervention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic pain syndrome involves widespread pain with prominent psychological, behavioural, and functional components without a primary nociceptive focus. MPS has identifiable peripheral nociceptive generators (trigger points) that can be successfully treated. The distinction matters because MPS responds to physical interventions while chronic pain syndrome requires a biopsychosocial management framework.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated or refractory MPS can progress to generalized chronic pain syndrome with central sensitization; overlapping pathophysiology and management.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: MPS and fibromyalgia share a spectrum of sensitization; MPS with widespread trigger points can evolve into fibromyalgia, and fibromyalgia patients frequently have active trigger points.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Paraspinal and quadratus lumborum trigger points are among the most common MPS presentations; regional back pain from MPS is one of the most prevalent occupational diagnoses globally.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Myofascial referred pain can mimic neuropathic distributions; distinguishing peripheral nerve sensitization from myofascial referral requires electrodiagnostic testing.

faqs.question: What is myofascial pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Myofascial pain syndrome is a condition where hyperirritable knots called trigger points develop within skeletal muscle, causing local and referred pain, restricted movement, and muscle tenderness. It is one of the most common diagnoses in pain and rehabilitation clinics, accounting for up to 85% of musculoskeletal pain presentations in some settings.

faqs.question: What are trigger points?
faqs.answer: Trigger points are palpable, painful nodules within a taut band of skeletal muscle. They produce the patient's familiar pain when pressed and refer pain to predictable distant sites. A local twitch response — a brief visible muscle contraction when the band is snapped — is a specific finding. They form from overuse, poor posture, or acute muscle strain.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myofascial pain syndrome and fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Myofascial pain syndrome is regional, with discrete trigger points that produce specific referred pain patterns and respond to local treatment. Fibromyalgia is widespread, affecting four or more body regions with generalized tenderness, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms but no discrete trigger points. The two conditions frequently co-occur in the same patient.

faqs.question: Where do trigger points most commonly occur?
faqs.answer: The upper trapezius, levator scapulae, sternocleidomastoid, infraspinatus, and cervical paraspinal muscles are the most commonly affected in the upper body, often causing neck pain, shoulder pain, and headache. The quadratus lumborum, gluteus medius, and piriformis are most common in the lower body, causing low-back pain and pseudo-sciatica.

faqs.question: How is myofascial pain syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical. A trained examiner palpates for a taut band with a hypertender nodule that reproduces the patient's referred pain when pressed. There is no blood test or imaging that directly confirms MPS. Imaging and nerve conduction studies are used to exclude radiculopathy, inflammatory disease, and fracture.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for trigger points?
faqs.answer: Trigger-point injection with dilute local anaesthetic and dry needling both have strong randomized-trial evidence for reducing VAS pain scores by 30–50% at 4 weeks. Dry needling is as effective as injection in most comparative trials. Combining needling with stretching, manual therapy, and exercise provides more durable relief than any technique alone.

faqs.question: Is dry needling the same as acupuncture?
faqs.answer: Both use similar filiform needles but differ in rationale. Dry needling targets anatomically identified taut-band trigger points based on musculoskeletal anatomy. Traditional acupuncture follows meridian theory and targets acupuncture points. Both techniques have evidence for reducing myofascial pain, though the mechanisms likely overlap.

faqs.question: Can myofascial pain syndrome cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Upper trapezius trigger points reliably refer pain to the temporal region, producing a tension-type headache. Sternocleidomastoid trigger points can cause forehead pain, behind-the-ear aching, and even dizziness. Treating the responsible cervical and trapezius trigger points is an established headache management strategy.

faqs.question: Can myofascial pain syndrome cause sciatica-like symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gluteus minimus, piriformis, and quadratus lumborum trigger points refer pain down the leg in patterns that closely mimic L5 or S1 radiculopathy. This is sometimes called pseudo-sciatica. The distinction is made by examination and nerve conduction testing: MPS shows no neurological deficits and normal nerve conduction.

faqs.question: How long does myofascial pain syndrome last?
faqs.answer: Acute MPS from a single injury or overuse episode typically resolves within 4–8 weeks with treatment. Chronic MPS lasting more than 3 months requires a multimodal program of 3–6 months to achieve significant sustained improvement. Perpetuating factors such as poor posture, vitamin D deficiency, or sleep disorders must be corrected for recovery to occur.

faqs.question: What exercises help myofascial pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Gentle aerobic activity (walking 30 minutes daily), targeted passive stretching of affected muscle groups (60–90 seconds per stretch, twice daily), and progressive eccentric-loading exercises are the most evidence-supported. Exercises should begin at low intensity and progress gradually — aggressive stretching of active trigger points initially worsens pain.

faqs.question: Does vitamin D deficiency cause myofascial pain?
faqs.answer: Vitamin D deficiency (serum 25-OH vitamin D below 20 ng/mL) is found in a significant proportion of chronic MPS patients and acts as a perpetuating factor by impairing muscle metabolic function and pain-threshold regulation. Correcting deficiency with supplementation (2,000–4,000 IU daily) improves trigger-point treatment response in multiple cohort studies.

faqs.question: Can stress cause myofascial pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic psychological stress elevates sympathetic tone, increases muscle bracing, and lowers the activation threshold of trigger points. Up to 60% of chronic MPS patients have comorbid anxiety or depression. CBT, relaxation training, and sleep improvement are integral to a complete MPS management program.

faqs.question: How many trigger-point injections can I have?
faqs.answer: There is no strict upper limit for local anaesthetic trigger-point injections, but most clinicians perform 3–5 sessions at 2–4 week intervals before reviewing response. Botulinum toxin is reserved for those who fail two courses of local anaesthetic. Sessions are spaced to allow physiotherapy and home exercise to consolidate gains between treatments.

faqs.question: Can myofascial pain syndrome be cured permanently?
faqs.answer: Complete permanent resolution is achievable in acute MPS with a clear precipitant and no perpetuating factors. Chronic MPS requires ongoing activity modification, ergonomic correction, and periodic treatment for most patients. Those who eliminate perpetuating factors — postural dysfunction, nutritional deficiencies, sleep disorders — have the best chance of long-term remission.

metaTitle: Myofascial Pain Syndrome: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Myofascial pain syndrome: trigger points in muscles cause regional and referred pain. Diagnosis, dry needling, injections and exercise treatment reviewed here.

sources.title: Simons DG, Travell JG, Simons LS. 1999. Travell and Simons' Myofascial Pain and Dysfunction: The Trigger Point Manual, Vol 1 (2nd ed). Williams & Wilkins, Baltimore.
Fernandez-de-Las-Penas C, Dommerholt J. 2018. International consensus on diagnostic criteria and clinical considerations of myofascial trigger points. Pain Med 19(1):142-150.
Morihisa R, Eskew J, McNamara A, Young J. 2016. Dry needling in subjects with muscular trigger points in the lower quarter: a systematic review. Int J Sports Phys Ther 11(1):1-14.
Shah JP, Thaker N, Heimur J, et al. 2015. Myofascial trigger points then and now: a historical and scientific perspective. PM R 7(7):746-761.
Ga H, Koh HJ, Choi JH, Kim CH. 2007. Intramuscular and nerve root stimulation vs lidocaine injection to trigger points in myofascial pain syndrome. J Rehabil Med 39(5):374-378.
Jeyaseelan T, Thomson S. 2021. Chronic pain. In: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, London.
Bron C, Dommerholt JD. 2012. Etiology of myofascial trigger points. Curr Pain Headache Rep 16(5):439-444.
